title: Calathea Leaves Curling? Here's How to Fix It Fast
excerpt: Calathea leaves curling is one of the most common signs your plant is stressed. Learn the exact causes and simple fixes to bring your calathea back to life.
content:
Why Are My Calathea Leaves Curling?

If you've noticed your calathea leaves curling inward, you're not alone. Calatheas are notoriously expressive plants — they'll let you know immediately when something isn't right. The good news? Curling leaves are your plant's early warning system, and with a little detective work, you can usually fix the problem quickly.

In this guide, we'll walk through every possible cause of calathea leaf curl, how to identify which one is affecting your plant, and exactly what to do about it.

The Most Common Causes of Calathea Leaves Curling

1. Underwatering

The number one reason calathea leaves curl is lack of moisture. When a calathea doesn't get enough water, it curls its leaves inward to reduce the surface area exposed to air — essentially trying to conserve whatever moisture it has left.

Check the soil before anything else. If the top two inches feel dry or the soil is pulling away from the edges of the pot, your calathea is thirsty.

  • Water thoroughly until it drains from the bottom of the pot
  • Allow the top inch of soil to dry out between waterings, but never let it dry completely
  • Consider a consistent watering schedule — calatheas love routine

2. Low Humidity

Calatheas are tropical plants native to the rainforests of South America, where humidity regularly hits 80–90%. Most homes sit around 30–50%, which can cause chronic leaf curling even if your watering routine is perfect.

If the edges of the leaves are also browning along with curling, low humidity is likely the culprit.

  • Use a plant humidifier near your calathea for the best results
  • Group your calathea with other plants to create a humidity microclimate
  • Place the pot on a tray filled with pebbles and water (just don't let the pot sit in the water)
  • Avoid misting directly — it can encourage fungal issues and doesn't raise humidity consistently

3. Temperature Stress and Cold Drafts

Calatheas are sensitive to temperature fluctuations. Cold drafts from windows, air conditioning vents, or exterior doors can cause leaves to curl rapidly. Similarly, temperatures below 60°F (15°C) will stress your plant significantly.

Check where your calathea is sitting. Is it near an AC vent? A drafty window? Move it to a stable, warm spot where temperatures stay between 65–80°F (18–27°C) consistently.

4. Overwatering and Root Rot

While underwatering is more common, overwatering can also cause calathea leaves to curl. When roots are waterlogged and begin to rot, they can no longer transport water and nutrients effectively — which ironically causes the plant to show the same drought-stress symptoms.

Signs that overwatering (rather than underwatering) is to blame include:

  • Soil that stays wet for more than a week after watering
  • A musty smell coming from the pot
  • Yellowing leaves alongside the curling
  • Soft, dark roots when you check the root ball

If you suspect root rot, remove the plant from its pot, trim any black or mushy roots with clean scissors, and repot in fresh, well-draining soil.

5. Water Quality Issues

Calatheas are famously sensitive to the minerals and chemicals found in tap water — particularly fluoride and chlorine. These can build up in the soil over time and cause leaf curl, browning tips, and general decline.

Switch to one of these alternatives:

  • Filtered water — a simple pitcher filter works well
  • Rainwater — calatheas absolutely love it
  • Distilled water — the gold standard but less sustainable long-term
  • Tap water left overnight — allows chlorine to dissipate, though it won't remove fluoride

6. Too Much Direct Sunlight

Calatheas prefer bright, indirect light. If your plant is getting direct sun exposure — even for just a few hours — the leaves may curl and develop crispy, bleached patches as they try to protect themselves from the intense light.

Move your calathea to a spot with filtered or indirect light. An east-facing window is often ideal. If your only option is a bright south or west window, use a sheer curtain to diffuse the light.

7. balanced liquid fertilizer Burn

Over-fertilizing or using a fertilizer that's too concentrated can cause salt buildup in the soil, which draws moisture away from the roots. This leads to leaf curl and brown tips that look similar to underwatering symptoms.

Stick to a diluted, balanced liquid fertilizer (at half the recommended strength) applied once a month during the growing season (spring and summer). Avoid fertilizing in fall and winter when growth slows.

How to Diagnose the Problem: A Step-by-Step Approach

  1. Check the soil moisture first — stick your finger two inches deep. Dry? Water it. Soggy? Hold off and check roots.
  2. Assess the humidity — grab an inexpensive hygrometer to measure your home's humidity levels.
  3. Consider the location — is the plant near a vent, window, or heater? Move it if so.
  4. Review your watering habits — are you using tap water? Watering inconsistently?
  5. Look at the whole picture — yellowing suggests overwatering or root rot; browning tips with curling often points to low humidity or water quality.

Preventative Care: Keeping Calathea Leaves Flat and Healthy

The best treatment is prevention. Once you've dialed in your calathea's environment, maintaining consistent conditions is key. Here's a quick summary of ideal care conditions:

  • Light: Bright, indirect light — no direct sun
  • Water: Filtered or rainwater, when the top inch of soil is dry
  • Humidity: 60% or higher when possible
  • Temperature: 65–80°F (18–27°C), away from drafts
  • Soil: Well-draining mix with good moisture retention
  • Fertilizer: Half-strength liquid fertilizer, monthly in spring and summer only

Will Curled Calathea Leaves Recover?

In most cases, yes. Once you identify and correct the issue, calathea leaves will often unfurl within 24–48 hours. Some older, severely curled leaves may not fully recover, but new growth should come in looking healthy and flat.

Be patient — calatheas can take a little time to settle after stress. As long as you see new leaves emerging, your plant is on the right track.

Related Care Topics Worth Exploring

If you're caring for a calathea, you might also find it helpful to read our guides on why calathea leaves turn yellow, how to tackle calathea root rot, and our full breakdown of best humidity-loving houseplants to pair with your calathea. If you're dealing with pests alongside leaf curl, check out our article on common houseplant pests and how to treat them naturally.

Calatheas reward attentive care with stunning foliage — once you crack the code, they're genuinely one of the most satisfying plants to grow indoors. You've got this!
